FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study baseline data (Release 2018), n=4524 initially, n=3847 after QC, 9-10 year olds in US

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time defined as 'average minutes per week on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' - unspecified platform, generic internet use without device/platform breakdown. Per B5 and B7, unspecified-platform use does NOT meet criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple machine learning regression approaches: ridge, LASSO, elastic net, and linear regression with FDR correction. Penalized regression coefficients (LASSO/ridge/elastic-net) count per prompt. Coefficients reported for all approaches.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports R² (0.12), RMSE (15.97, 17.34), variable importance coefficients in figures (Fig. 1, 2, 3); linear regression reports p=7.5×10^-8 for screen time

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining associations of 43 demographic, psychological, behavioral, cognitive variables with BMI using multiple analytical approaches on training/test split data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research paper, no indication of duplicate/preprint status

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply because FT_IC2 is NOT_MET. EC7 criteria only relevant when all IC criteria (including IC2) are MET.
notes: Paper fails FT_IC2 (screening criterion) due to unspecified, generic screen time without qualifying smartphone or social media platform specificity. Thus paper is NOT_MET overall and does not proceed to full-text inclusion regardless of IC3-IC6 status.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline cross-sectional survey (Release 2.0), n=11,000+ US children age 9-10 across 21 sites, epidemiologically-informed school-based recruitment

FT_IC2.evidence: Reports separate qualifying variables: 'social media', 'texting', 'video chat' as child-reported items on typical weekend/weekday use. Per B1, YSTS papers (Youth Screen Time Survey or equivalent) reporting separate β/OR for SM, texting, video chat meet criteria regardless of whether TV/gaming also reported. All three meet definition of qualifying SM (social media, texting on phone, video chat on phone).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analyses examining associations between screen media types and sleep disturbance symptom severity; reports odds ratios (e.g., 'OR 1.3' for weekend screen use and child-reported suicidality); reports p-values and associations with sleep outcomes (sleep duration, sleep onset latency, insomnia, excessive sleepiness)

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports numeric statistics: odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals, p-values throughout results section. Abstract mentions 'OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7' for weekend screen use association with sleep disturbances.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining associations of screen media use (different types) with sleep disturbance symptom severity outcomes in baseline cross-sectional data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research paper, preregistered study, no indication of duplicate status

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply per condition (a): smartphone/SM is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting sleep disturbance outcomes, not the DEPENDENT variable. Paper examines SM → sleep disturbance, not demographics → SM. EC7 requires SM as DV in EVERY analysis involving SM.
notes: PAPER MEETS all FT_IC1-IC6 criteria. FT_EC7 does not apply because SM is IV (predictor), not DV. Paper qualifies for inclusion.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Curated Annual Release 2.0; sample of 7,994 unrelated children, mean age 9.9 years (SD 0.5), from ABCD cohort of 11,875 children aged 9-10 at baseline

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'weekend screen use time' as a predictor of suicidality. Separately reports family conflict, parental supervision, and school involvement as other predictors. Screen time here is qualifying variable per B5 exception - paper reports 'higher weekend screen use time (OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7)' as an independent predictor with its own coefficient.

FT_IC3.evidence: Uses 'bootstrapped logistic regression' to quantify associations between suicidal ideation/behaviors and multiple predictors including screen use; reports odds ratios with 95% CIs and statistical significance

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports numeric statistics: odds ratios (e.g., OR 1.7-4.8 for child psychopathology; OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7 for weekend screen use; OR 0.8, 95% CI 0.7-0.9 for parental supervision); percentages of suicidal ideation/plans/attempts

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining risk and protective factors (including screen time) for childhood suicidality using bootstrapped logistic regression on ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed research article, appears to be original work with no indication of duplicate status

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply per condition (a) and structure: screen/media use is one of many independent variables predicting suicidal ideation/behaviors (outcomes). SM is IV, not DV. Paper is cross-sectional, not longitudinal (required for EC7 condition c). EC7 only applies when SM is DV in ALL analyses.
notes: PAPER MEETS all FT_IC1-IC6 criteria. Screen use measured with separate coefficient as predictor of suicidality outcomes. Cross-sectional design. FT_EC7 does not apply (SM is IV, not DV). Paper qualifies for inclusion.
